claims.claim_text: A named 2023 healthcare startup ran 42 microservices, 180 pods, and three node groups on EKS for $18,000 per month.
claims.anchor_text: In 2023, a healthcare startup ran their entire platform on EKS -- 42 microservices, 180 pods, three node groups. Their monthly Kubernetes bill was $18,000.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A startup can end up running many always-on services on EKS and discover that the aggregate monthly bill is much higher than several low-traffic services justify.
claims.rationale: This is a named, dated, quantified anecdote with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: Eleven services handled under 100 requests per day, one PDF generator was called exactly 23 times daily yet ran two pods 24/7, and another service sat idle 23 hours per day.
claims.anchor_text: During a cost optimization sprint, an engineer noticed that 11 of those microservices handled fewer than 100 requests per day. One service -- the PDF report generator -- was called exactly 23 times per day but required 2 pods (for HA) running 24/7. Another service processed insurance eligibility checks at 3 AM, running idle for 23 hours daily.
claims.suggested_rewrite: During cost review, teams often find low-traffic or time-windowed services that still require always-on pods and therefore spend most of their time idle.
claims.rationale: These are fake-precise operational details inside the same unsourced war story.

claims.claim_text: Moving those services to Lambda cut monthly cost from $4,200 to $38 while 31 services remained on EKS.
claims.anchor_text: They moved those 11 services to AWS Lambda. The combined monthly cost dropped from $4,200 to $38. Not a typo -- thirty-eight dollars. The remaining 31 services stayed on EKS, where their steady-state traffic justified always-on compute.
claims.suggested_rewrite: They moved the lowest-traffic, intermittently used services to Lambda while keeping steadier services on EKS, which is a common hybrid pattern when some workloads benefit from scale-to-zero and others benefit from always-on compute.
claims.rationale: Dollar amounts and exact service counts in an unattributed migration story must be removed.

claims.claim_text: Serverless is favored below 1M requests per month and Kubernetes above 10M requests per month.
claims.anchor_text: | **Request volume** | < 1M requests/month | > 10M requests/month |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Request volume** | Low or bursty request volume | High steady request volume |
claims.rationale: These thresholds are illustrative teaching numbers, not durable cross-provider breakpoints.

claims.claim_text: Serverless should be favored only for executions under 15 minutes.
claims.anchor_text: | **Execution duration** | < 15 minutes | Long-running processes |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Execution duration** | Short-lived request or event handlers | Long-running processes |
claims.rationale: An exact 15-minute boundary is not a stable cross-provider serverless rule.

claims.claim_text: Acceptable cold starts for serverless are typically 100-500 ms.
claims.anchor_text: | **Cold start tolerance** | Acceptable (100-500ms) | Unacceptable (real-time APIs) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Cold start tolerance** | Some startup latency is acceptable | Tight latency budgets make startup delay unacceptable |
claims.rationale: Cold-start timing varies widely by runtime, image size, provider, and mitigation strategy.

claims.claim_text: Serverless and Kubernetes cost roughly the same at about 2M requests per month.
claims.anchor_text: - **~2M requests/month**: serverless and K8s cost roughly the same
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Cost crossover point**: the break-even point depends heavily on request duration, memory, and the provider's pricing model
claims.rationale: This is an illustrative crossover number rather than a generally citable fixed threshold.

claims.claim_text: Serverless is always cheaper at low volume and Kubernetes is always cheaper at scale.
claims.anchor_text: The exact crossover depends on execution time, memory, and provider pricing. But the general shape is always the same: serverless is cheaper at low volume, Kubernetes is cheaper at scale.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The exact crossover depends on execution time, memory, provider pricing, and how efficiently you can run containers. Serverless often wins for spiky low-duty-cycle workloads, while always-on container platforms often become more attractive for steadier demand.
claims.rationale: The absolute phrasing overstates a context-dependent cost trade-off.

claims.claim_text: Knative manages scale-to-zero, revisions, and traffic splitting for serverless-style workloads on Kubernetes.
claims.rationale: The primary source is the official Knative documentation, but `knative.dev` is not on the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: `min-scale: "0"` by itself means true serverless scale-to-zero.
claims.anchor_text: - `min-scale: "0"` -- Scale to zero when idle (true serverless)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - `min-scale: "0"` -- Sets the lower replica bound to zero; with scale-to-zero enabled, the revision can scale down fully when idle
claims.rationale: The annotation is only part of the behavior and the current wording overstates it.

claims.claim_text: `target: "10"` means each pod handles exactly 10 concurrent requests before scaling out.
claims.anchor_text: - `target: "10"` -- Each pod handles 10 concurrent requests before scaling out
claims.suggested_rewrite: - `target: "10"` -- Tells the autoscaler to aim for roughly 10 in-flight requests per pod when using concurrency-based autoscaling
claims.rationale: This is an autoscaling target, not a strict per-pod guarantee.

claims.claim_text: The table's billing-unit comparison is uniformly accurate across EKS Fargate, GKE Autopilot, and AKS Virtual Nodes.
claims.anchor_text: | Billing unit | Per pod (vCPU + memory per second) | Per pod (vCPU + memory per second) | Per container group (ACI pricing) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Billing unit | Workload-level compute charges | Workload-based billing in many common cases | Container-group-level charges via the underlying platform |
claims.rationale: The row over-compresses three different pricing models and misses important platform-specific exceptions.

claims.claim_text: DaemonSets are unsupported on EKS Fargate and AKS Virtual Nodes, while GKE Autopilot supports them since 2024.
claims.anchor_text: | DaemonSets | Not supported | Supported (since 2024) | Not supported |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | DaemonSets | Node-level agent patterns need extra care | Standard Kubernetes patterns are available | Node-level agent patterns need extra care |
claims.rationale: The dated qualifier is unsupported here and the row should avoid uncited version-history detail.

claims.claim_text: GPU support is available on EKS Fargate and GKE Autopilot but not AKS Virtual Nodes.
claims.anchor_text: | GPUs | Supported (limited) | Supported | Not supported |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | GPUs | Specialized hardware support is more constrained than on node-backed clusters | Supported for suitable workloads | Specialized hardware support is limited |
claims.rationale: The row is too specific and is incorrect as written for EKS Fargate.

claims.claim_text: Persistent storage support lines up cleanly as EBS CSI on Fargate, GCE PD on Autopilot, and Azure Files on Virtual Nodes.
claims.anchor_text: | Persistent storage | EBS CSI (since 2024) | GCE PD | Azure Files |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Persistent storage | More constrained than on node-backed clusters | Standard Kubernetes storage patterns are available | More constrained than on node-backed clusters |
claims.rationale: This row collapses materially different storage limitations into an inaccurate one-line comparison.

claims.claim_text: Cold-start startup times are typically 30-60 seconds on EKS Fargate and 15-30 seconds on AKS Virtual Nodes.
claims.anchor_text: | Startup time | 30-60 seconds | Transparent | 15-30 seconds |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Startup time | Startup latency can be noticeable | Startup behavior is mostly abstracted by the managed platform | Startup latency can be noticeable when bursting into external capacity |
claims.rationale: These timings are environment-dependent benchmarks presented as fixed expectations.

claims.claim_text: Pods that match a namespace or namespace-plus-label selector in a Fargate profile are scheduled onto Fargate automatically.
claims.rationale: The EKS Fargate profile docs explicitly describe namespace and label selector matching for Fargate scheduling.

claims.claim_text: Lambda provisioned concurrency eliminates cold starts for the pre-provisioned level of concurrency.
claims.rationale: AWS's launch write-up directly explains that provisioned concurrency removes the normal cold-start setup path for pre-initialized environments.

claims.claim_text: Lambda SnapStart gives a 90% reduction for JVM cold starts.
claims.anchor_text: | **SnapStart** (Lambda Java) | Snapshot after init, restore on invocation | 90% reduction for JVM cold starts |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **SnapStart** (Lambda Java) | Snapshot after init, restore on invocation | Can reduce startup latency substantially, sometimes to sub-second levels |
claims.rationale: AWS documents substantial startup-latency reduction, but not this fixed percentage claim.

claims.claim_text: GraalVM native images start Java in 10-50 ms.
claims.anchor_text: | **GraalVM native images** | Ahead-of-time compilation | 10-50ms startup for Java |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **GraalVM native images** | Ahead-of-time compilation | Can reduce Java startup time substantially for some workloads |
claims.rationale: The exact timing range is highly workload- and environment-dependent.

claims.claim_text: AWS Lambda processes over 10 trillion invocations per month as of 2025.
claims.rationale: AWS's own Lambda recommendation page states that customers generate over 10 trillion invocations per month.

claims.claim_text: Firecracker can start a new microVM in under 125 milliseconds.
claims.rationale: The Firecracker FAQ explicitly states a startup time below 125 ms.

claims.claim_text: Knative was created by Google, Pivotal, and IBM in 2018, is now a CNCF Incubating project, and Cloud Run is essentially managed Knative under the hood.
claims.anchor_text: 2. **Knative was originally created by Google, Pivotal, and IBM in 2018** to bring serverless to Kubernetes. It is now a CNCF Incubating project and forms the basis of Google Cloud Run, which is essentially managed Knative under the hood.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 2. **Knative emerged as an open serverless layer for Kubernetes** and helped popularize portable, container-based serverless patterns across Kubernetes environments and managed platforms.
claims.rationale: This sentence mixes partly outdated governance/status details with an oversimplified Cloud Run characterization.

claims.claim_text: GKE Autopilot charges for pod resource requests rather than limits, so over-requesting directly increases cost.
claims.anchor_text: 3. **GKE Autopilot charges you for pod resource requests, not limits**, which means over-requesting CPU or memory directly increases your bill. This pricing model forces teams to be precise about resource requests -- a good habit that most teams on standard Kubernetes ignore.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **Autopilot billing follows the resources your workloads ask for in many common cases**, so over-requesting CPU or memory can directly increase your bill.
claims.rationale: The module overgeneralizes across Autopilot pricing modes and product behaviors.

claims.claim_text: A longest-running 2024 Lambda example processed satellite imagery for the full 15-minute timeout, and before the 2018 limit increase from 5 to 15 minutes the team had to chain six functions.
claims.anchor_text: 4. **The longest-running Lambda function in 2024 ran for the full 15-minute timeout** processing satellite imagery for a climate research project. Before the 2018 limit increase from 5 to 15 minutes, the team had to split their pipeline into six chained functions -- a painful reminder that serverless time limits shape architecture.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **Per-invocation time limits shape serverless architecture**, so long-running work often needs decomposition, background queues, or a different execution model.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced anecdote with fake-precise timing, date, domain, and team behavior.

claims.claim_text: Knative system components generally require dedicating 2-3 nodes.
claims.anchor_text: | Running Knative on a cluster without enough baseline capacity | Knative's activator and autoscaler need resources | Dedicate 2-3 nodes to Knative system components |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Running Knative on a cluster without enough baseline capacity | Knative's activator and autoscaler need resources | Reserve enough baseline capacity for Knative system components instead of assuming scale-from-zero removes all platform overhead |
claims.rationale: The exact node count is an unsupported environment-specific sizing rule.

claims.claim_text: EKS Fargate does not support DaemonSets.
claims.rationale: The EKS Fargate documentation explicitly lists DaemonSets as unsupported.

claims.claim_text: The SQS message-size limit relevant to Lambda triggering is 256 KB.
claims.anchor_text: | Ignoring the 256 KB message size limit when triggering Lambda from SQS | Works in dev with small payloads | Store large payloads in S3; pass only the S3 key through the queue |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Ignoring the message size limit when triggering Lambda from SQS | Works in dev with small payloads | Keep queue messages within the current service limits; store large payloads in object storage and pass only a reference through the queue |
claims.rationale: The stated limit is outdated and should be rewritten without the false precise number.

claims.claim_text: Async Lambda invocations should use a dead-letter queue or on-failure destination.
claims.rationale: AWS documents both dead-letter queues and destinations for failed asynchronous invocations.

claims.claim_text: API Gateway response caching should be enabled broadly for read-heavy endpoints.
claims.anchor_text: | Using API Gateway as a pass-through without caching | Every request hits the backend | Enable API Gateway response caching for read-heavy endpoints |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Using API Gateway as a pass-through without caching | Every request hits the backend | Enable gateway-layer caching where your API product and traffic pattern actually support it |
claims.rationale: Caching support and trade-offs vary by gateway product and configuration, so the advice is too absolute as written.

further_reading.title: AWS Lambda asynchronous invocation
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers queueing behavior, async invocation semantics, and error-handling context for Kubernetes-to-Lambda handoff patterns.

further_reading.title: Knative Serving repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Good upstream entry point for Knative Serving concepts such as scale-to-zero, revisions, and request-driven compute.

further_reading.title: Google Kubernetes Engine pricing
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for understanding where Autopilot pricing follows Pod requests and where hardware-specific cases use different billing.

notes: No shared citation pool exists for this section, so `section_pool_ref` is null. Quiz and hands-on exercise content were intentionally excluded per pipeline rules; claims here cover the expository prose and factual table rows only.
